title: Politics in a 'post-truth' era
explanation:
Politics in a 'post-truth' era

Some commentators argue that emotional appeal now rivals factual evidence in public debate. Social media amplifies sensational claims and allows false stories to spread before they can be corrected. Defenders of the term 'post-truth' say it describes a structural shift, not mere lying. Others reject the label as elitist, claiming voters still care about evidence but distrust institutions that present it. Either way, democratic debate depends on media literacy and transparent verification.

questions.text: Emotional appeal may now rival [*factual evidence|sports results|weather forecasts only].
Social media helps false stories spread [*before correction|after everyone forgets|only in print].
Democratic debate depends on media literacy and [*transparent verification|secret voting only|luxury travel].

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: Sensational claims spread quickly on social ____.  | *media | menus | museums
Some critics call the label ____.  | *elitist | tiny | tasty
Voters may distrust the ____ that present evidence. | *institutions | instruments | ingredients
False stories can spread with little immediate ____.  | *correction | collection | connection
The writer presents [*multiple perspectives|no argument|only jokes].
The passage concerns [*public discourse|garden design|train schedules].
The tone is [*concerned but measured|angry throughout|purely comic].
